mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes SLK250 or SLK350 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned SLK engine typically costs £3,000–£6,000 supply only depending on which engine you have. Used M271 engines are available from approximately £1,500–£3,500. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines are no longer available.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes SLK250 or SLK350 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine you have. The M271 (SLK250) is notorious for timing chain elongation and camshaft adjuster oil leaks that can damage the ECU. The M272 V6 (SLK350) is a smooth, refined engine, but early models (pre-2008) are prone to balance shaft gear failure that can lead to terminal timing chain failure. Later M272 engines (post-2008) and R172 generation SLK350s are significantly more reliable. With proper maintenance, these engines can be very durable.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes SLK250 and SLK350?

acceptedAnswer.text: SLK250 (M271): Timing chain elongation and tensioner failure (rattling on cold start), camshaft adjuster failure, oil leaks from camshaft adjuster solenoids that can damage the ECU, blue smoke on startup, HPFP issues, and ignition coil failures. SLK350 (M272): Balance shaft gear failure on early models (pre-2008) — check engine light, rough running, rattle, intake manifold tumble flap failure, and oil leaks.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes SLK engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £15,000 SLK350, a £5,000 recon is 33% — worth it. Against a tired £5,000 example, the same recon approaches our 70% Mercedes threshold — get a scrap valuation first. Well-maintained SLKs are appreciating — a proper restoration is often justified for a clean example.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes SLK250 or SLK350 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. SLK250 (2011–2016): M271 DE18 AL. SLK350 (2004–2016): M272. The badge on the tailgate and the generation (R171 or R172) are the easiest tells — 'SLK 250' for the four-cylinder turbo, 'SLK 350' for the V6.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the timing chain on the M271, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative timing chain and tensioner replacement costs £800–£2,000. The M271 is notorious for timing chain elongation, often caused by a failing tensioner. If the chain stretches beyond tolerance or the tensioner fails, the consequences range from misfires and cam/crank correlation codes to catastrophic engine damage if the chain jumps a tooth or snaps. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes using MB 229.5 spec oil are the single most effective preventative measure. For the early M272, balance shaft replacement is a more complex repair costing £1,500–£4,000.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes SLK250 / SLK350 Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: No longer available from Mercedes — these engines are out of production

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the M271 and M272 petrol engines. Used M271 engines are available from approximately £1,500–£3,500. A full engine replacement can run anywhere between £1,500 and £5,000 on average. Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200 supply only. Main dealer new engines are no longer available.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an SLK engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent) or £120–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The M272 V6 requires more labour than the M271 four-cylinder.

problems.title: Timing Chain Elongation & Tensioner Failure — M271 (SLK250)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking from the engine, check engine light (P0016), rough idle, flat spots, misfires, hard starting.
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,000 (chain + tensioner + cam adjuster replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £3,500–£7,500 (if chain snaps and valves strike pistons)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M271 engine is notorious for timing chain elongation, often caused by a failing tensioner. If the chain stretches beyond tolerance or the tensioner fails, the consequences range from misfires and cam/crank correlation codes to catastrophic engine damage if the chain jumps a tooth or snaps. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the chain and tensioners inspected immediately. The camshaft adjusters (intake and exhaust) are also known to fail. Regular oil changes using MB 229.5 spec oil are essential.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: Balance Shaft Gear Failure — Early M272 (SLK350 pre-2008)
problems.symptoms: Check engine light, rough running conditions, stalling, loud rattle or slapping noise from the engine, OBD trouble codes P1200 and P1208.
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£4,000 (balance shaft gear replacement + timing chain)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,500 (if balance shaft failure leads to engine damage)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear a loud rattle or slapping noise, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: The M272 V6 engine in early SLK350 models (pre-2008) is known for a poorly manufactured gear on the balance shaft. Since the balance shaft gear is driven by the timing chain, when it fails, the timing of the engine will change. Early signs are misfiring and the 'check engine' light — followed by terminal timing chain failure. Correction is replacement of the balance shaft or balance shaft gear, along with the timing chain and tensioner. A class action settlement may cover some repair costs on select vehicles. If you notice any symptoms, have the engine inspected immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full balance shaft failure guide

problems.title: Oil Leaks from Camshaft Adjuster Solenoids — M271 (SLK250)
problems.symptoms: Check engine light, engine management issues, oil residue on cam sensors and harness plugs, eventual ECU failure.
problems.repairCost: £200–£600 (camshaft adjuster solenoid replacement + harness cleaning)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: Many Mercedes SLK engines suffer from a known engineering flaw where oil leaks from the camshaft adjuster solenoids into the electrical connectors. This fluid travels through the wiring harness via capillary action, eventually reaching and damaging the Electronic Control Unit (ECU). If oil saturates this sensitive hardware, it often leads to total engine failure and triggers the engine management light. Regularly inspect your cam sensors and harness plugs for any signs of dampness or oil residue. If you find oil, act immediately.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: SLK250 / SLK350 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches the fault before catastrophic failure
Class action may cover some costs; catch it early
Catch it before oil damages the ECU
Repair kit available; catch it early
Oil feed bolt kit resolves the issue
Typically requires full engine replacement or complete rebuild

buyingChecks.title: Buying This SLK? Check These First
buyIf: * SLK250: Post-2013 build (revised timing chain components); full service history with MB 229.5 spec oil
* SLK350: Post-2008 M272 (balance shaft issue resolved) or R172 generation (2011–2016)
* No cold-start rattle — a quiet startup suggests healthy timing chain (M271) or balance shaft (M272)
* Camshaft adjuster solenoids and harness plugs inspected and free from oil
avoidIf: * M271: Cold-start rattle with no timing chain history
* M272 pre-2008: Check engine light, rough running, or rattle with no balance shaft history
* Any engine: Oil residue on cam sensors or harness plugs (ECU damage risk)
* Poor service history — both engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The SLK250 and SLK350 represent two distinct approaches to the Mercedes roadster formula — the turbocharged four-cylinder M271 (efficient but timing-chain-sensitive) and the naturally aspirated V6 M272 (smooth but with early balance shaft concerns). The M271 is notorious for timing chain elongation and camshaft adjuster oil leaks that can damage the ECU. The early M272 V6 (pre-2008) has a well-documented balance shaft gear failure that can lead to terminal engine damage. Later M272 engines (post-2008) and R172 generation SLK350s are significantly more reliable. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained SLK is a £8,000–£20,000 future classic; a neglected one is a £5,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare SLK engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes SLK250 or SLK350 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling, ticking, or slapping (M271: timing chain; M272 pre-2008: balance shaft)
* Service History — including which engine (M271 or M272) and confirmed oil-change intervals using MB 229.5 spec oil
* Camshaft Adjuster Check (M271) — Check cam sensors and harness plugs for oil residue (ECU damage risk)
* Balance Shaft Check (M272 pre-2008) — Any check engine lights, rough running, or rattle? (balance shaft warning)
* Smoke Check — Blue smoke on startup indicates oil feed line issue (M271)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any timing, balance shaft, or cam adjuster codes?
* Roof Operation — Check the electric folding hard-top operation (common issue)
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
